observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the:
a) Municipal Accountant and GSO-designate to reconcile their records on PPE
and address the unreconciled difference of ₱51,421,794.74;
b) Municipal Accountant to examine the RPCPPE submitted, particularly the
PPEs reported in the physical count and ensure that those PPEs are
supported with subsidiary ledger cards and recorded in the books; and
c) GSO-designate, and in coordination with the Inventory Committee, to:
1) conduct an annual and complete physical inventory of all PPE items,
including local road networks and infrastructure assets; and
2) regularly update its records and prepare and maintain property cards
(PCs) for each class of PPE to record the acquisition, description,
custody, disposal, and other information about the asset.

We recommended that the LGU, through the Accounting Department:
a) Restate the reported balances of the various inventory account for CY 2022
and 2023 in the Statement of Financial Position, and Prior Period
Adjustments for CY 2023 in the Statement of Changes in Net Assets/Equity;
b) Submit the comparative Financial Statements in CY 2024 and CY 2023 as
restated reflecting the correction of error;
c) Provide an adequate note disclosure for the prior period adjustments, in
accordance with the provisions of IPSAS 3 in its Notes to Financial
Statements for CY 2024, specifying therein: 1) the nature of the prior period
error; 2) for each prior period presented, to the extent practicable, the
amount of correction for each financial statement line item affected; 3) the
amount of correction at the beginning of the earliest prior period presented;
and 4) if retrospective restatement is impracticable for a particular prior
period, the circumstances that led to the existence of that condition and
description of how and from when the error has been corrected; and
d) Submit a summary of proof of distribution on the reported inventory
expense account charged to prior years for audit verification.

We recommended that Management:
a) Direct the Municipal Treasurer and Accountant to account for the possible
overdeposit, unaccounted collections and/or erroneous recording that
caused the noted variance of ₱111,273.56 in the Cash-Local Treasury account
as at December 31, 2024, then effect the necessary adjustment in the books,
copy furnished the Audit Team;
b) Prospectively ensure the periodic reconciliation between the treasury and
accounting records to immediately correct any variance; and
c) Also direct the Municipal Accountant to provide the necessary Notes
Disclosure for the previous Municipal Treasurer’s accountability totaling
₱231,328.28; otherwise, consider the reclassification thereof to the proper
Receivable account with necessary Notes Disclosure in order to fairly present
the Cash-Local Treasury account prospectively.

We recommended that Management:
a) Formally demand within May 2025 to the LGU’s depository banks the copy
of unreceived Bank Statements (BS) as basis of the preparation of Bank
Reconciliation Statements, and restate the banks’ obligation to furnish BS
to the Accountant within five days after the end of each month, as prescribed
by COA Circular No. 96-011;
b) Direct the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal
Treasurer and Budget Officer, to assess which among the 20 LGU bank
accounts are necessary to be maintained, then cause the proper closure of
those determined to be inactive or have no purpose, subject to applicable
procedures; and
c) Provide additional accounting personnel who will assist the Municipal
Accountant in the preparation of periodic LGU reports, like BRS, in
compliance with the cited Circular, which directs the preparation of
monthly BRS for all LGU bank accounts and subject to reconciliation and
subsequent adjustment of valid book reconciling item/s. This is to achieve
the fair presentation of the LGU’s Cash in Bank accounts in its financial
statements.

We recommended that the Management direct the following:
a) Municipal Assessor to:
1) Periodically reconcile records with the Municipal Treasurer at least
annually to ensure that records are properly updated and to facilitate
the establishment of correct RPT and SET receivables at the beginning
of the year; and
2) Work on electronically linking the records of the Municipal Assessor and
Municipal Treasurer’s for easier access and reconciliation.
b) Municipal Treasurer to:
1) Update the RPTAR/RPTIC based on the submitted Assessment Roll
from the Municipal Assessor’s Office;
2) Prepare the summary list of RPT and SET receivables based on the
updated RPTAR/RPTIC and submit it together with the list of taxpayers
and their corresponding amount of taxes to the Office of the Municipal
Accountant at year-end or the beginning of the succeeding year to be the
basis for setting the receivable;
3) Carefully review the summary and the detailed reports on the RPT and
SET Receivables before their submission to the Office of the Municipal
Accountant to ensure that the receivables set up at the beginning of the
year reflect the true estimate of receivables for the period; and
4) Maintain a record of taxpayers with unpaid taxes, and regularly update
the report based on the RPTAR/RPTIC. Subsequently, reconcile, at least
annually, with the Office of the Municipal Accountant to determine the
correct balances of the RPT and SET receivables;
c) Municipal Accountant to:
1) Review the entries made in the books, particularly on December 2024
based on the supporting documents attached, and draft the correcting
entry to update the balance of the receivable;
2) Analyze and review the supporting reports submitted by the Office of
the Municipal Treasurer to confirm the correctness of the data presented
in the summary report and reconcile your records with the Office of the
Municipal Treasurer on the unpaid RPT and SET to properly establish
and substantiate the reported receivables in the book; and make the
necessary adjustments; and
3) Ensure that the recorded RPT and SET receivables accurately reflect the
balance of receivables at year- end after careful review and analysis to
avoid understatement or overstatement of receivables and
misrepresentation of the financial statements as a whole.

We recommended that Management:
a) Immediately direct the concerned finance and implementing LGU
offices to exert collaborative effort in the preparation of Loan Utilization
Report as of December 31, 2024, commencing from the approval date of
the two loan agreements;
b) Formally confirm with the lending bank within May 2025 the non-
release of the loan proceeds for Items II.a and II.b totaling
₱20,742,000.00, and if warranted, secure the document/s evidencing such
directive and approved amendment to the Term Loan Agreement 1, copy
furnished the Audit Team; otherwise, this portion of the loan transaction
may be issued with Notice of Suspension; and
c) Ensure that internal control is in place for the LGU loans to ensure that
these loans are effectively managed, government resources are
safeguarded from wastage, prudence is exercised in contracting future
loan agreements and loans payable account is fairly presented in the
financial statements.

We recommended that Management:
a) Except for the loan amortizations allowed to be charged against the 20 per
cent Development Fund (DF), stop appropriating separately under the 20
per cent DF for the high-cost, long-term undertakings that are already
financed by the term loans in the same fund, particularly for the noted
procurement of lands;
Otherwise, any additional appropriations out of the DF may be
suspended/disallowed in audit for being irregular and unnecessary;
b) Avoid charging barangay-allocated projects that may properly be financed
by Barangay’s own funds in order to optimally utilize the same for other
development projects by the Municipality for the benefit of the general
public;
c) Reassess the going concern of the distribution of jetmatic pumps and
streetlights materials; otherwise prioritize other capital expenditures in the
DF appropriations (for CY 2025 and onwards), the benefits of which extend
beyond the fiscal year, and add to the assets of the government; and
d) Ensure that projects, programs and activities (PPAs) funded out of the DF
must be “well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready” by
incorporating specific PPA titles and description as to the nature of
transaction, location/s and purpose.

We recommended that management:
a) Direct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and BAC-Technical Working
Group (-TWG), for the LGU projects in CY 2025 and onwards, to identify
the proper eligibility requirements specific to the nature of the goods or
service to be procured, and ensure that the bidder meets all the eligibility
requirements;
b) Undertake the review or evaluation of the build and design of the motorized
steel boat, and, should a modification or alteration be proper, ensure that
Item VIII.4 of MARINA Circular No. 2022-02 is strictly complied with, and
further evaluate whether the additional expenses due to the modification or
alteration are properly chargeable against the original contractor;
c) Seek proper registration of its watercrafts before the MARINA (Note:
Should an administrative fine be imposed by the latter against the LGU, such
fine, regardless of amount, cannot be charged against LDRRMF or any local
funds); and
d) Ensure that the LGU-owned-and-operated river boats are adequately
equipped at all times with the minimum life-saving, communication, and
safety equipment.

We recommended that Management:
a) Refrain from charging expenses not directly related to DRRM against the
LDRRMF, such as the fuel costs incurred for the use of MDRRM vehicles
for purposes other than for response and rescue/relief activities;
b) Also stop using the MDRRM rescue vehicles for purposes other than for
response and rescue/relief activities;
c) Recommended to the Sanggunian the creation of an Ordinance authorizing
the use of specific government vehicles upon the request of private persons
or entities for purposes, such as but not limited to burial/funeral, wedding,
teambuilding, and prescribing guidelines therefor;
d) Revert back the amount of ₱21,000.00, representing the cost of pre-
engineering activity, to the LDRRMF-Trust Fund account;
e) Instruct the LDRRM Council to prepare, review, and update annually its
LDRRMFIP, and include therein the list of projects and activities to be
charged against the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years in the special
trust fund account of the agency;
f) Ensure the preparation of the revised AIP, if the unexpended LDRRMF will
be utilized for new expenditure items, and observe the proper authorization
for the release and use of LDRRMF through the preparation of Budget, and
enactment of an Appropriations Ordinance; and
g) Direct the MDRRM Officer to utilize the LDRRMF strictly for the
implementation of projects and activities exclusively for disaster prevention,
mitigation, preparedness, response, rehabilitation and recovery.

We recommended that management:
a) Allocate funds for the construction of perimeter fence, and playground
facility for the NCDC, and ensure their prompt construction so that the
NCDC will be used for its intended functions; and
b) Also regularly allocate funds to support the NCDC’s implementation and
operations, including repairs and maintenance, power, utilities, security,
and other operating expenses.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a.) Prepare adjusting journal entries necessary to record the amount under the
appropriate liability and equity accounts as prescribed in Chapter 10 of the
PMP Project Operations Manual; and
a) Recognize the receipt of the next tranches of PBG, if any, as an outright
income, and the subsequent utilization thereof under appropriate asset and
expense accounts in compliance with the provisions of the Operations
Manual.

We recommended that Management:
a) Direct the Municipal Budget, Accounting and other LGU Offices on the
immediate assignment and use of the assigned Responsibility Center (RC)
and their corresponding RC Code for the agency’s GAD Focal Point System
starting CY 2025 in order to account, monitor and ensure that the expenses
to be charged are related to the approved GAD Plan and Budget (GPB);
b) Also direct the Municipal Accounting Office to establish and maintain a
separate Subsidiary Ledger for GAD-related expenses to easily facilitate the
preparation of a prompt and reliable GAD Accomplishment Report; and
c) Assess the possible attribution of GAD in the proposed GPB items for the
succeeding period and subject them to the “Harmonized Gender and
Development Guidelines” (HGDG) Checklist, in order to avoid inaccurate
reporting of the whole transaction amounts in the GAD AR that may result
to excess over the approved GPB.
Otherwise, the excessive GAD expenditure may be disallowed in audit.

We recommended that Management:
a) make use of the designated Special Disbursing Officer at the MSWDO to
handle cash advance for AICs (financial assistance) and not only the
educational assistance;
b) direct MSWDO through issuance or incorporating it in the Manual of
Operations being adopted by the LGU that financial assistance to indigent
beneficiaries be given in the form of outright cash, instead of direct payment
in check to facilitate the distribution of assistance to needy clients at the
earliest possible time;
c) ensure that policies adopted by the MSWD are aligned with the pertinent
provisions of DSWD issuances on social welfare and development; and
d) direct the MSWDO to coordinate with DSWD Region or National on how
the LGU can avail of AICs program being implemented by the Region or
National to obtain additional fund for the LGU’s AICs program.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal
Engineer to implement a sound and effective monitoring system in place to compel
contractor’s compliance of the requirements of Section 62 of the RIRR of RA 9184 in
order to ensure that the procuring entity will be reimbursed of its expenses in the
restoration or reconstruction works in case of structural defect/failure occurring
during the applicable warranty period.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a) Require the Municipal Engineer and/or concerned authorized agency
personnel to supervise the implementation of the infrastructure projects,
and submit to the audit team the copies of the Results of Test Analyses/Status
of Quality Control Test, with certified xerox copy of official receipt as
required by the aforementioned rules and regulations, in order to ensure
that the completed projects conform with agency standards, plans and
specifications as part of its quality control program;
b) Require the Municipal Engineer/Bids and Awards Committee to include
Minimum Materials Testing Equipment in the Technical Component of the
Bid, together with the other major equipment as contained in the Technical
Envelope, which shall be subjected to preliminary examination and further
verified during the post-qualification stage; and
c) Henceforth, require the Municipal Accountant not to pass during pre-audit
of the transaction particularly on final payment, disbursement vouchers on
infrastructure projects that are not supported with Results of Test Analyses
from DPWH/BRS/DOST/DTI accredited testing laboratories for
construction materials and concrete which require testing to avoid
suspensions and/or disallowances in audit.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the:
(a) The Municipal Accountant and GSO-designate to reconcile their records
on PPE and address the unreconciled difference of ₱103,386,197.51. The
Municipal Accountant ensure that PPE items recorded in the books are
supported with PPELCs and property cards on the part of the GSO.
(b) Municipal Accountant to:
1) Review its records and determine the components of unidentified PPE
items of ₱68,425,533.02 reported in the books against the Lapsing
Schedule;
2) Record all acquisition, disposal, or transfer of PPEs in the PPELCs
and ensure that the total balance of PPELCs tally with the balances of
controlling PPE accounts in the General Ledger; and
3) Examine the RPCPPE submitted particularly those PPEs reported in
the physical count and ensure that those are supported with property
cards on the part of the GSO-Office and recorded in the books in the
part of the Accounting.
(c) GSO-designate, with the supervision of the Municipal Treasurer, and in
coordination with the Inventory Committee to:
1) Conduct an annual physical inventory of all PPE items, including local
road networks and infrastructure assets;
2) Regularly update its records and prepare RPCPPE and RPCLRN
following the format prescribed in Annex 49 of NGAS for LGUs, and
Annex C of COA Circular 2015-008, respectively; and
3) Prepare and maintain PCs for each class of PPE to record the
acquisition, description, custody, disposal, and other information
about the asset.

We recommended that Management:
a) Follow the provisions on the Manual for NGAS on Perpetual Inventory
System for proper inventory control;
b) Submit the Relief Distribution Sheets, or Liquidation Reports, and
pictures for the distribution to end-users/beneficiaries of the food,
medicines, and medical supplies costing ₱1,826,531.41 and
₱1,203,992.13.
c) Direct the Municipal Accountant to prospectively:
1. Ensure that there are RIS and SSMI together with distribution sheets
and pictures that would substantiate the issuance and distribution of
inventories before adjusting it to the appropriate expense account in
the books;
2. Request from GSO-designate an inventory count of current
inventories on hand and reconcile it with the records to adjust the
purchases that were treated as outright expenses and restore it in the
inventory account if there are remaining inventories on hand;
3. Maintain a supplies ledger cards per commodity/stock as required by
the guidelines and periodically reconcile it with the stock cards and
property cards to be maintained by the GSO for checks and balances;
d) Direct the GSO-designate to prospectively:
1. Maintain the property cards and stock cards and periodically
reconcile it with the ledger cards by the Accounting Department;
2. Ensure that requisition of stocks is supported with RIS for supplies
carried in stock or PR for supplies not carried in stock, as appropriate,
and summarize it into SSMI weekly to be submitted to the
Accounting Office for the drawing of appropriate entries;
e) Direct the concerned offices to prepare Relief Distribution Sheets, and
Liquidation Reports specifying clearly on the face of the document the
following information: (1) the document title; (2) the date; (3) type/s and
quantity of relief goods received by each beneficiary; and (4) name,
address, and signature of the beneficiary, and submit the same
immediately to the GSO-designate and/or Accounting Office to enable
them to update their records; and
f) The Inventory Committee, to conduct the semestral count and prepare
and submit the RPCI to the Audit Team not later than July 31 and
January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters,
respectively.

We recommended that the Management direct the:
a) Municipal Treasurer to:
1) Closely coordinate and reconcile with the Municipal Assessor’s Office on
the timely submission of the Assessment Roll reflecting the updated 2016
General Revision of real properties within the Municipality’s jurisdiction;
2) Update the RPTAR/RPTIC based on the submitted Assessment Roll from
the Municipal Assessor’s Office;
3) Prepare the summary list of RPT and SET receivables based on the
updated RPTAR/RPTIC and submit it together with the list of taxpayers
and their corresponding amount of taxes to the Office of the Municipal
Accountant at year-end or the beginning of the succeeding year to be the
basis for setting the receivable;
4) Carefully review the summary and the detailed reports on the RPT and
SET Receivables before their submission to the Office of the Municipal
Accountant to ensure that the receivables set up at the beginning of the
year reflect the true estimate of receivables for the period; and
5) Maintain a record of taxpayers with unpaid taxes, and regularly update
the report based on the RPTAR/RPTIC. Subsequently, reconcile, at least
annually, with the Office of the Municipal Accountant to determine the
correct balances of the RPT and SET receivables.
b) Municipal Assessor to:
1) Regularly update and timely furnish the Office of the Municipal
Treasurer with the Assessment Roll and other transactions affecting the
assessment and recognition of real properties in the Municipality; and
2) Periodically reconcile records with the Municipal Treasurer at least
annually to ensure that records are properly updated and to facilitate the
establishment of correct RPT and SET receivables at the beginning of the
year.
c) Municipal Accountant to:
1) Review the entries made in the books, particularly on December 2023
based on the supporting documents available, and draft the correcting
entry to update the balance of the receivable;
2) Analyze and review the supporting reports submitted by the Office of the
Municipal Treasurer to confirm the correctness of the data presented in
the summary report and reconcile records with the Office of the Municipal
Treasurer on the unpaid RPT and SET to properly establish and
substantiate the reported receivables in the book, and make the
necessary adjustments; and
3) Ensure that the recorded RPT and SET receivables accurately reflect the
balance of receivables at year- end after careful review and analysis to
avoid understatement or overstatement of receivables and
misrepresentation of the financial statements as a whole.

We recommended that Management:
a) Direct the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to review the existing designation
order for the Liquidating Officer, and issue a new or supplemental Order
thereto with the specific duties, procedures, and prescribed forms/reports
and deadlines in compliance with the prescribed guidelines on Manual for
NGAs on LGUs;
b) Also direct the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to conduct re-orientation to the
LO and collectors/tellers in the MTO, including the deputized Barangay
Treasurers and Local Civil Registrar who issue Community Tax
Certificates (CTC) (BIR Form No. 16) and Marriage License Certificate
(A.F. No. 54), respectively, about the preparation of sufficient number of
copies of their RCDs, to keep one on file, and timely remittance to the LO
of collections; and
c) We likewise recommended that Management consider the appointment of
the present OIC-Municipal Treasurer (from June 2020) to a plantilla
position.
Lastly, the Audit Team would like to commend the Municipal Treasurer for
significantly improving the internal control system in the Municipal
Treasurer’s Office to safeguard the LGU collections and encourage the same
to continue improving the system based on the prescribed guidelines.

We recommended that Management prospectively:
a) Secure appropriate authorization specifically, (1) Ordinance authorizing the
LCE or vice-LCE to augment any item in the approved annual budget for
their respective offices from savings in other items within the same expense
class of their respective appropriations and (2) LBE form 2 (Augmentation
Form) of the Budget Operations Manual duly prepared by the Budget
Officer, certified correct by the Accountant and approved by the LCE/ vice
LCE, as appropriate; and
b) If the LGU, in cooperation with law enforcement agencies intends to use
CF, it should follow the guidelines set forth by the JMC Circular No. 2021-
001 for its entitlement, release, use, reporting, and audit of confidential
and/or intelligence funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to:
a) Trace, review, and analyze the long-standing balances that are still
reported as Due to NGAs, and Due to LGUs, and make appropriate
actions to settle or correct such balances, such as, (i) retrieve the MOAs,
(ii) together with the implementing and other concerned offices,
coordinate with the SAs, submit the liquidation reports, and return any
excess funds, and (iii) draw the necessary adjusting/correcting entries;
b) Maintain a separate file compiling the copy of the MOAs for every fund
transfer or grant received by the Municipality for easier tracing of the tenor
of the fund transfer;
c) Make the necessary adjusting/correcting entries to reclassify the
PhilHealth reimbursements, and the balance from the SK-related
activities implemented by the Municipality amounting to ₱559,252.83,
and ₱29,818.65 that are erroneously accounted as Due to NGAs, and Due to
LGUs, respectively;
d) Determine the amounts reimbursable to the barangays for their payments
for the CTC forms, coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer to cause the
corresponding refund to the barangays, and make the necessary adjusting
entry to de-recognize the liability for the remainder;
e) Ensure the completeness and propriety of supporting documents in the
payment of honoraria; and
f) Submit to the auditors and/or attach to the Journal Entry Vouchers the
relevant supporting documents, such as the MOA, and
Utilization/Liquidation Reports duly received by SA, in relation to the
actions undertaken to liquidate its outstanding liabilities.
We also recommended that the management, headed by the Municipal
Mayor, enter into a Memorandum of Agreement with the barangays for the
barangay counterparts received by the Municipality to set clear guidelines in
the receipt, implementation, reporting, and liquidation of said funds.

We recommended that Management:
a) Reassess the LGU’s current planning, budgeting, and implementation flow and
documentation procedures and determine points of improvement to ensure
that the development projects, including those already appropriated for this
year, are “well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready” within
CY 2024;
b) Direct the Local Development Council to indicate more specific PPA titles,
locations, and target beneficiaries, to have close coordination from
planning until implementation, and to avoid redundant PPAs, unless deemed
as a priority;
c) Subject to the faithful determination of the Bids and Awards Committee
(BAC) on the proper mode of procurement, consider public bidding
particularly for the goods in the Provision of Potable Water System and
Electrification Program, to avail of the most advantageous terms to the
government;
d) Prospectively submit proofs of distribution to resident beneficiaries and
proof of turn-over to barangay recipients; and
e) Comply with the required quarterly Monitoring report with a complete list
and consistent details thereon, along with other monitoring tools, such as the
maintenance of a separate registry for DF transactions.

We recommended that Management:
a) Prepare and submit a detailed Loan Utilization Report (per project
component) concerning the 2021 Term Loan Agreement;
b) Present to the Audit Team the overall plans with detailed implementation
timelines, financing framework, and income- generating strategy of each
of the project components financed by the 2021 term loan agreement; and
c) Prospectively ensure that loans entered into by the LGU are effectively
managed and that prudence is exercised in contracting future loan
agreements.
We likewise recommended (a), (b), and (c) above to substantiate the
additional Term Loan Agreement of ₱100,000,000.00 for the establishment of a
Public Cemetery in Canaman.

We recommended that Management:
a) Except for the allowable DF loan amortizations, stop appropriating
separately under the 20 percent DF for the high-cost, long-term
undertakings that are already financed by the 2021 term loan costing
₱98,358,000.00, particularly for the site development with embankment
and procurement of lands;
b) Present to the Audit Team the overall plans with detailed implementation
timelines, financing framework, and income- generating strategy of each
of the project components financed by the CY 2021 term loan agreement;
c) Direct the Local Development Council (LDC) to revisit the LGU’s existing
CDP-LDIP and CLUP to incorporate the overall plan of the identified
priority projects, duly reflective of the current circumstances in the
locality;
d) Also direct the LDC, in cooperation with the Sanggunian, to reassess the
approved CY 2024 appropriation for DF and effect necessary
adjustments, such as that in Recommendation (a) above; and
e) Prospectively ensure that development projects are “well-planned and
procurement-and-implementation-ready”.

We recommended that Management through the efforts of the Local Chief
Executive and Sangguniang Bayan:
a) Prioritize the conduct of a comprehensive study or careful assessment of
the current status of the water concern of the constituents in the Locality;
b) Designate an Office or competent point person/focal person to oversee the
LGU’s assessment of the current circumstances on the water supply
concern in all 24 Barangays (subject to regular reporting to the local chief
executive and sanggunian);
c) After the assessment has been completed, consider exploring possible
options or alternatives on whether to (1) create the ‘complete waterline
loop’ in coordination with the local water utility nearest the Municipality;
(2) form its own water district through the initiative of the local
sanggunian in cooperation with Local Water Utilities Administration
(LWUA) per Presidential Decrees on Water Utilities Act; or (3) explore
other alternatives depending on the results of the careful assessment of the
situation;
d) If option c(1) has been decided on, retrieve the relevant communications
and minutes of past legislative inquiries with the nearest water local
utility, to account for the history and latest update thereon; and designate
a point person or office to make representation and/or send LGUs formal
communication to the nearest local water utility to resume the joint
deliberation and determination of the best, feasible course of action to
address the persisting and pressing concern of the water supply systems
in Canaman; and
e) If option (c)2 has been chosen, initiate a dialogue and coordinate with
LWUA as well as with other neighboring water districts to know the
requisites and other necessary courses of action in forming the
Municipality’s own water district.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a) Direct the Municipal DRRM Council (MDRRMC) to:
i. Fast- track the updating and approval of the LDRRMP for the years
2020 to 2025;
ii. Set timelines for the preparation of the subsequent LDRRMPs;
iii. Include the unexpended DRRMF for the past five years in its annual
LDRRMFIP to fund the continuation of partially implemented and
unimplemented DRRM PPAs, or submit a revised AIP recommending
new expenditure items for the approval of the Sangguniang Bayan and
LCE; and
iv. Increase the utilization rate of DRRMF through proper planning, and
effective implementation of planned PPAs;
b) Direct the MDRRMO, Municipal Budget Officer, and Municipal
Accountant to:
i. Specify in the activity proposal and/or the Allotment and Obligation
Slip (ALOBS) the exact PPA as described in the LDRRMFIP against
which the cost of such activity would be applied;
ii. Refrain from charging expenses not directly related or responsive to
DRRM against the LDRRMF; and
iii. Monitor and evaluate the proper charging of actual costs incurred as
against the PPAs identified in the LDRRMP so that total

PPA;
c) Recommend to the Sanggunian the creation of an Ordinance authorizing the
use of specific government vehicles upon the request of private persons or
entities for purposes, such as but not limited to burial/funeral, wedding,
teambuilding, and prescribing guidelines therefor.

We recommended that Management revisit its Municipal Ordinance No.
2018-193 and its Solid Waste Management (SWM) Plan, then institute legal
mechanisms/ administrative sanctions to strictly implement the mandated
prohibition on the use of non-environmentally acceptable packaging and the
banning of the use of unnecessary single-use plastics in the local government
offices and other establishments within the LGU’s jurisdiction, following
Section 30 of R.A. No. 9003 and NSWMC Resolution No. 1363 series of
2020, to contribute to the national government’s efforts of reducing waste
generation at source.

We recommended that the Management:
a) Ensure that at least five percent of its Annual Budget is allocated to GAD by
allocating a separate GAD fund to support the identified GAD-focused PPAs
in the GPB and/or, in case of GAD attribution from the Municipality’s
regular/flagship PPAs funded by other sources, by conducting the
appropriate HGDG checklist; and
b) Direct the lead or implementing offices to accomplish and submit the
appropriate HGDG checklist (i.e., design checklist, funding facilities
checklist, or generic checklist for the GPB, and PIMME checklist or
FIMME checklist for the AGDAR) together with the supporting
documents (e.g., project proposal/brief, accomplishment report, and

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the:
Municipal Accountant to:
Update its records and determine the components of unidentified PPE items of ₱261,105,349.16 reported in the books; and
Record all acquisition, disposal or transfer of PPEs in the PPELCs and ensure that the total balance of PPELCs tally with the balances of controlling PPE accounts in the General Ledger.
GSO-designate, with the supervision of the Municipal Treasurer, and in coordination with the Inventory Committee, if already created to:
Conduct an annual physical inventory of all PPE items, including local road networks;
Update its records and prepare RPCPPE and RPCLRN following the format prescribed in Annex 49 of NGAS for LGUs, and Annex C of COA Circular 2015-008, respectively; and
Prepare and maintain PCs for each class of PPE to record the acquisition, description, custody, disposal and other information about the asset.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
OIC-Municipal Treasurer to:
Designate a liquidating officer to verify the correctness and completeness of the Report of Collections and Deposits, Accountable Forms, and moneys turned over by the collectors/tellers;
Deposit all collections intact and daily or not later than the next banking day; and
Provide or fill out all required information in the cashbook, make correcting and/or adjusting entries, and reconcile his records with the Accounting’s in order to maintain a correct and reliable cashbook balance;
Municipal Accountant to:
Make the necessary correcting entries for the identified errors amounting to ₱21,094.50, which overstated the Cash-Local Treasury account; and
Trace, analyze, and correct possible errors in recording that caused the unreconciled differences between the books and the cashbook, and conduct periodic reconciliation of records with the Treasury; and
Former Municipal Treasurer to deposit her undeposited collections totaling ₱189,517.54 balance still outstanding as of December 2021 (previously communicated in an AOM last year).

We recommended that Management require the Petty Cash Custodian to: (a) limit the disbursements out of the PCF to petty operating expenses that cannot conveniently and practically be paid by check; (b) stop charging the fuel, oil and lubricants expenses, and professional fees to the PCF, and consider more acceptable mode of payment, which is direct payment by check; and (c) control/regulate the monthly petty cash expenses within established PCF caps per month to avoid unreasonable excesses that indicate inefficiency and lack of prudence in the handling of the Funds.

We recommended that Municipal Mayor issue a policy to stop the manual payment of wages, payroll, allowances and incentives of the employees and job orders, instead, it shall be through the same existing Automated Payroll System of paying monthly salaries and wages.  Likewise, facilitate the direct crediting of the same to employees’ bank accounts to prevent delay and avoid the risk of loss or misappropriation of public funds that are inherent in a manual system.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to:
Conduct regular reconciliation of reciprocal accounts to ensure that discrepancies are immediately identified, reconciled and adjusted;
Prepare necessary correcting entries to reflect the correct balances of the accounts; and
Eliminate the reciprocal accounts in the consolidated financial statements.

We recommended that Management:
Trace back the warranties totaling ₱452,318.14, which have been outstanding beyond the warranty period to its original entries, supporting documents and related purchases, and release or forfeit such warranties, as appropriate; and
Comply with the conditions set forth in Section 62 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 in the release of warranties to concerned suppliers or contractors, in order to avoid the undue withholding or premature release of such warranties.

We recommended that the Management:
Direct the Municipal Treasurer to:
Release and distribute the share of each barangay in the proceeds of basic Real Property Tax (RPT) and community tax for CY 2021 as soon as reconciliation with Accounting Department is completed; and
Prospectively, ensure the distribution of barangay shares in the collection of RPT and community tax within the periods cited above; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to designate back-ups who would perform the tasks assigned to absent personnel to prevent the stoppage and/or delay in the operations within the Department.

We recommended that Management:
Conduct a comprehensive evaluation of the operations and financial management system of the Public Market, and Birthing Clinic and Clinical Laboratory to evaluate whether they are financially viable to continue operating as LEEs;
If the LGU intends to continue operating the said LEEs, institutionalize the establishment of LEE through an Ordinance proposed by the LCE, supported by a Feasibility Study and 5-Year Business Plan, and duly enacted by the Local Sanggunian; and
Maintain a special account for each LEE in the General Fund, in compliance with the LEE Manual, and Local Government Code, and comply with all other requirements for the establishment and creation of LEE.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Engineer to prepare and submit the detailed cost of components of infrastructure projects of existing public infrastructure assets and reconcile the information with the records of the Office of the Municipal Accountant to ensure the correctness and completeness of the report; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Recognize depreciation expense for all depreciable assets, including the depreciable components of infrastructure assets to present the correct valuation of the PPE accounts;
Record the depreciation expense for infrastructure assets upon submission by the Office of the Municipal Engineer of the detailed list of components of infrastructure assets; and
Record any changes as adjustments in the ensuing year to reflect the correct balance of accumulated depreciation for each item of PPE.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer to:
deposit immediately to the authorized depository bank all her undeposited collections as of December 31, 2019 and strictly comply with Section 28 of COA Circular No. 92-382 in succeeding transactions; and
coordinate with Accounting Department to determine the cause of the negative balances of the Cash in Treasury account for the current and prior years.

We recommended that Management direct the immediate reconciliation of the corresponding cash balances maintained by the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to ensure correctness and reliability of the reported cash balances as of December 31, 2019.

We recommended that Management require the concerned departments to:
exert extra efforts in the collection of the loan balance of ₱649,511.55; and
reassess whether the loans have served its purpose by monitoring the sustainability of the livelihood program extended to the beneficiaries.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the other concerned officials particularly the Municipal Engineer, to provide all the details and information needed to determine the particulars and cost of each item recorded in the RPIs.

We likewise recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to thereafter, take up the necessary journal entries to recognize in the books the public infrastructure assets totaling ₱54,103,924.03, as well as the related accumulated depreciation and impairment losses, if any, in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-004, to present fairly the value of the assets in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management:
create a committee composed of regular personnel who shall conduct on a full time basis the: (1) physical count of all properties and submission of a complete and consolidated report; and (2) disposal of the unserviceable properties to avoid further deterioration; and
require the property officer to designate an employee who shall maintain Property Ledger Cards for each PPE account on a full time basis,  to facilitate periodic reconciliation with the accounting records; and issue Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment (ARE) for all properties to pinpoint accountability.

We recommended that Management:
direct the implementing department in coordination with the accounting and treasury department to return the unused balance for completed projects to its source agencies to fully liquidate and settle their account; and
monitor the status of projects funded by national agencies and timely coordinate with the source agency to ensure that any grants be settled accordingly.

We recommended that Management require the Accountant to monitor due dates and make prompt payments thereof to avoid incurring additional ten per cent (10%) penalty on overdue accounts otherwise the amount shall be disallowed in audit.

We recommended and Management agreed to strictly adhere to the requirements enumerated under COA Circular No. 2012-001 regarding the transfer of funds to other agencies to ensure the propriety and validity of the transactions.  Moreover, it is recommended that the LGU adequately monitor its subsidies to other LGUs to warrant that said transfers are used exclusively for public purposes and not wasted.

We recommended that Management:
delegate an employee who will be assigned to prepare, monitor and submit the required Monthly Fuel Consumption Report; and
ensure that the forms, records, and reports prescribed under COA Circular No. 77-61, on the reporting and control over fuel consumptions, are prepared, submitted and properly accomplished so that wasteful, excessive and unnecessary expenditures are minimized and disbursements are effectively controlled and properly accounted for.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer:
to submit to the Municipal Accountant the subject DVs who shall thereafter submit it to COA for audit, to forestall the issuance of Notice of Suspension; and
for internal control purposes, stop the practice of allowing claimants to have possession of paid Disbursement Vouchers to prevent misplacement of the same, and if it could not be avoided, monitor the timely return of said vouchers.

We recommended that Management refrain from awarding contracts to suppliers who are not registered with PhilGEPS, to ensure a more open, transparent and competitive environment with regard to procurement of goods and services.

Management ensure compliance with the PhilGEPS registration requirement and further assured that for any mode of procurement, including shopping and small value procurement, supplier’s PhilGEPS registration will be considered before recommending award and issuance of the purchase orders or contracts.

We recommended that Management always require the contactor to post surety bond in accordance with the required threshold to guarantee correction of structural defects and/or failures that may arise during the warranty period for the project.
We recommended that Management ensure that all EDF projects proposed and funded per approved Annual Investment Plan of the LGU be implemented within the budget year.

We recommended that Management:
before leasing out a market stall or space, execute a Contract of Lease with the lessee to put into writing the agreements, and to better protect the rights and obligations of each contracting party; and
strictly impose the 25% surcharge and 24% interest per annum should the lessee fail to pay his or her rent on the twentieth (20th) day of the current month without the need of demand to ensure prompt collection.
